---
title: "Where does the data come from?"
subtitle: "Reproducible data management for big neuroimaging"
format: revealjs
---

## {.center}

<br>

### Where does the data come from?

#### Reproducible data management for big neuroimaging

<br>

**AS4SAN Workshop**

::: notes
Welcome. Before we get into analysis pipelines, models, and results — I want to
talk about the step that comes before all of that. Where does the data actually
come from, and how do you keep track of it?
:::

---

## Every other module assumes the data is already here

<br>

> "Download the dataset and place it in `./data/`"
>
> — Every tutorial ever

<br>

- Preprocessing pipelines assume the files exist
- Analysis scripts assume a specific directory layout
- Papers reference "the dataset" but not *which version*

. . .

**Nobody talks about how the data got there — or whether it's the same data you had last week.**

::: notes
This is the gap. Every other part of the workshop — the preprocessing, the
statistical analysis, the machine learning — all of it starts with "assuming
you have the data." But getting the data, knowing which version you have,
and being able to reproduce that exact starting point — that's the hard part
nobody talks about.
:::

---

## Does this look familiar?

<br>

```
my_study/
├── data_final/
├── data_final_v2/
├── data_FINAL_really/
├── analysis_old.R
├── analysis_new.R
├── analysis_new_fixed.R
├── results_fig1_BACKUP.png
└── README_ignore_this.txt
```

<br>

- Which version of the data produced which figure?
- What changed between "final" and "final_v2"?
- Could a colleague reproduce your analysis from this folder?

. . .

**Version control solves this — for code *and* data.**

::: notes
I think most of us have been here. You start organised, but six months into a
project you have five copies of the data and three versions of the analysis
script and no idea which combination produced the figure in the paper. This is a
version control problem. We have good tools for this — let me introduce the one
that matters most.
:::

---

## A quick intro to git

<br>

**git** is a tool that tracks changes to files over time — like "track changes" in Word, but for entire folders.

<br>

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**What git gives you:**

- A complete history of every change
- The ability to go back to any previous version
- A record of *who* changed *what* and *when*
- Safe collaboration — no overwriting each other's work

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**The basic cycle:**

```bash
# 1. Make changes to your files

# 2. Stage — choose what to save
git add my_analysis.R

# 3. Commit — save a snapshot
git commit -m "Fix outlier filter"

# 4. Push — share with collaborators
git push
```

:::
::::

::: notes
If you haven't used git before, here's the one-minute version. Git tracks
changes to files in a folder. Every time you make a meaningful change, you take a
snapshot — called a commit — with a short message describing what you did. You
can always go back to any previous snapshot. And when you collaborate, git
makes sure nobody accidentally overwrites anyone else's work. Think of it like
track changes in Word, but it works on any file and keeps the full history
forever.
:::

---

## git is great — but it wasn't built for big data

<br>

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**git handles well:**

- Analysis scripts (R, Python)
- Participant lists, metadata (CSV, TSV)
- Documentation and notes
- Configuration files

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**git struggles with:**

- MRI scans (hundreds of MB each)
- Large datasets (GBs to TBs)
- Binary files that don't "diff" well
- Data stored on remote servers

:::
::::

<br>

. . .

**We need something that works *like* git, but can handle big neuroimaging data.**

That's where DataLad comes in.

::: notes
Git is fantastic for code and small files, but it was never designed for large
data. If you try to put a hundred NIfTI files into a git repository, it'll grind
to a halt. The files are too big, they're binary so git can't show you what
changed, and you'll run out of disk space fast. What we need is a tool that gives
us the same benefits — history, collaboration, reproducibility — but that can
handle the kind of data we actually work with in neuroimaging.
:::

---

## The concrete problem: MR-RATE

<br>

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

| | |
|---|---|
| **Patients** | 83,425 |
| **MRI volumes** | 705,254 |
| **Total size** | 8.1 TB |
| **Modalities** | T1, T2, FLAIR, SWI, MRA |
| **Extras** | radiology reports, brain masks, segmentations |

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**You can't just download 8 TB.**

- Too large for a single machine
- Evolving — new releases, corrections
- Multi-site (Zurich, Istanbul, NVIDIA)
- You only need a *subset* for your analysis

:::
::::

<br>

<small>Hugging Face: `Forithmus/MR-RATE` — CC BY-NC-SA 4.0</small>

::: notes
Let's make this concrete. MR-RATE is a large neuroimaging dataset — 83,000
patients, over 700,000 MRI volumes, 8 terabytes total. You can't just download
this to your laptop. And even if you could, the dataset changes — new data gets
added, errors get corrected. So how do you work with something like this
reproducibly? You need a system that lets you grab just the piece you need, and
keeps track of exactly which version you used.
:::

---

## DataLad: version control that works for data

<br>

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DataLad                        │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐   │
│  │     git      │    │    git-annex     │   │
│  │  tracks:     │    │  tracks:         │   │
│  │  • metadata  │    │  • large files   │   │
│  │  • scripts   │    │  • where to get  │   │
│  │  • history   │    │    them from     │   │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<br>

- **git** handles the small stuff (scripts, metadata, history)
- **git-annex** handles the big stuff (scans, volumes) — stores *pointers*, not the actual files
- **DataLad** wraps both so you don't need to think about the layers underneath

::: notes
DataLad is built on top of git. It adds a layer called git-annex that can handle
large files. The trick is that git-annex doesn't actually store your MRI scans in
the repository — it stores a pointer that says "this file exists and here's where
to get it." So you can have a dataset with thousands of scans, clone it
instantly, and only download the specific files you need. You don't need to
understand git-annex to use DataLad — DataLad gives you simple commands that
handle everything.
:::

---

## The core loop: install → get → run → rerun

<br>

| Step | Command | What happens |
|------|---------|-------------|
| **install** | `datalad install -s <url>` | Clone metadata — no large files yet |
| **get** | `datalad get sub-01/anat/*.nii.gz` | Fetch only the files you need |
| **run** | `datalad run -m "msg" -- <cmd>` | Execute & record what you did |
| **rerun** | `datalad rerun` | Reproduce from the recorded history |

<br>

<!--
  Asciinema cast embed placeholder.
  Replace demo/casts/demo.cast with your recorded cast, then use one of:

  Option A — asciinema-player (recommended):
    <div id="demo-player"></div>
    <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/asciinema-player@3/dist/bundle/asciinema-player.min.js"></script>
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/asciinema-player@3/dist/bundle/asciinema-player.min.css">
    <script>AsciinemaPlayer.create('demo/casts/demo.cast', document.getElementById('demo-player'));</script>

  Option B — fallback linked GIF:
    [![Demo](assets/demo.gif)](demo/casts/demo.cast)
-->
<p style="text-align:center; color:#888; border:2px dashed #ccc; padding:2em;">
  [ asciinema cast will be embedded here — see comments above ]
</p>

::: notes
This is the heart of DataLad — four commands that cover the whole workflow.
Install gives you the full directory tree and all the metadata, but none of the
large files — it's instant even for terabyte-scale datasets. Get fetches exactly
the files you need, nothing more. Run wraps any command you'd normally type and
records exactly what was run, on which inputs, producing which outputs — like a
lab notebook for your computer. And rerun replays that recorded command — same
inputs, same command, verifiable output. If a reviewer asks "can you reproduce
figure 3?" — you can.
:::

---

## Why it matters

<br>

### Provenance — your digital lab notebook

> "Which version of which data produced this figure?"

DataLad records the **exact command**, **inputs**, and **outputs** — automatically.

<br>

### Selective access — download only what you need

Get 50 MB of scans for your analysis, not 8 TB for the whole dataset.

<br>

### Collaboration — no more emailing files

Everyone works from the same dataset. The history shows who changed what and when.

::: notes
Three reasons this matters for your day-to-day research. First, provenance — you
can go back to any result and see exactly what data and what command produced it.
Think of it as a lab notebook for your computational work. Second, efficiency —
you only download what you actually need. If you're analysing 20 subjects from a
dataset of 83,000, you don't need to download the other 82,980. Third,
collaboration — when you're working with colleagues across sites, everyone works
from the same versioned dataset. No more emailing zip files or wondering if
you're looking at the same data.
:::

---

## On-ramp: getting started

<br>

```bash
# Install DataLad (if you haven't already)
pip install datalad
sudo apt install git-annex    # or: brew install git-annex

# Clone the workshop dataset (metadata only — fast)
datalad install -s https://github.com/forithmus/MR-RATE workshop-data

# Fetch the files you need for the exercises
cd workshop-data
datalad get exercises/
```

<br>

You now have exactly the files the notebooks expect — and a record of where they came from.

::: notes
Here's how you get set up for the rest of the workshop. Install DataLad and
git-annex if you don't have them — it's a one-line pip install. Then datalad
install the workshop dataset — this is fast because it only grabs metadata, not
the actual scan files. Then datalad get the exercise files you need. That's it.
Your notebooks will work, and you have a full trail of where every file came
from. If something goes wrong, you can always get back to exactly this state.
:::

---

## Pointers and resources

<br>

| Resource | Link |
|----------|------|
| **DataLad Handbook** | [handbook.datalad.org](http://handbook.datalad.org) |
| **OpenNeuro** | [openneuro.org](https://openneuro.org) |
| **DataLad datasets** | [datasets.datalad.org](http://datasets.datalad.org) |
| **MR-RATE** | [huggingface.co/datasets/Forithmus/MR-RATE](https://huggingface.co/datasets/Forithmus/MR-RATE) |
| **MR-RATE code** | [github.com/forithmus/MR-RATE](https://github.com/forithmus/MR-RATE) |
| **This talk** | [github.com/arush-arun/AS4SAN-Workshop](https://github.com/arush-arun/AS4SAN-Workshop) |

<br>

The DataLad Handbook is the single best starting point — it's a full tutorial with worked examples, no programming background required.

::: notes
Here are the key resources. The DataLad Handbook is excellent and written for
researchers, not software engineers — it walks you through everything from
installation to advanced workflows. OpenNeuro hosts thousands of neuroimaging
datasets that you can access via DataLad. The MR-RATE dataset we discussed is on
Hugging Face. And this talk itself is a git repository — the slides are version-
controlled markdown, so you can see exactly how they were built. Practice what
you preach.
:::
